narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your bar performance. For you specifically, it calculated a 48.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for rejection—meaning the school sees too high a risk of you not succeeding right away after graduation. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile. This low standing signaled to the model a potential struggle with advanced coursework that often correlates with bar challenges. Next in influence, your LSAT score of 35 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the average of 36.7 seen in successful students, showing solid reasoning ability. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 also helped somewhat, nudging things positively despite being under the 3.21 average, as it still reflects decent college performance. Other aspects like your high family income bracket and full-time student status were neutral or minor in the model's eyes, aligning with the norm for most applicants. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile stands out mainly due to that low Year 3 decile, which outweighed your strengths elsewhere. While this is disappointing, especially with your strong family support and near-average test scores, it highlights where the model sees the biggest gap. To move forward, focus on improving your academic decile in remaining years—perhaps through tutoring, study groups, or retaking key courses—to boost your profile for reapplication. You could also retake the LSAT to push it higher or gather letters emphasizing your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact areas, so this rejection is a clear roadmap rather than a dead end. Keep pursuing your legal ambitions; with targeted improvements, future models and admissions teams will likely see you differently.
